description: Essential emergency ultrasound cases. Focus on the most essential pathology and principles.

introVideos.title: Doppler Ultrasound Part 1 - Principles (w/ focus on Spectral Waveforms)
Doppler Ultrasound Part 2 - Spectral Waveforms from Head to Toe (Normal and Abnormal)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Gallbladder tensely distended, diameter > 4 cm (normal GB < 4 cm)
  • Cholelithiasis, with shadowing stones throughout, including near the neck
  • Gallbladder wall thickening (> 3 mm) and increased vascularity (normal wall < 3 mm)
  • Sonographic murphy's sign was positive (can be falsely negative with analgesia)
  • No biliary dilatation (CBD 4 mm)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Ultrasound is the first line imaging test to assess for acute cholecystitis, and is quite sensitive.

In acute cholecystitis, gallstones in the neck/cystic duct cause tense distension of the gallbladder, which becomes inflamed/infected.

On ultrasound, the presence of gallstones, tense distension of the gallbladder, and positive sonographic murphy's sign (maximum pain with probe pressure over the gallbladder) is already highly suspicious for cholecystitis. Secondary evidence, such as wall thickening, hyperemia, echogenic fat surrounding (inflammatory change), pericholecystic fluid, all make the diagnosis even more likely.

Complications to look for

As discussed on CT case of acute cholecystitis, you should look for complications. On ultrasound, look for:

  • Wall disruption (perforation)
  • Pericholecystic collections (perforation)
  • Gangrenous cholecystitis (sloughed membranes within the GB)
  • Emphysematous cholecystitis (gas within the wall -- look for dirty shadowing from gas, ring-down artifact)

teachingSections.name: Key Concept: Ddx Gallbladder Thickening
teachingSections.html:
A VERY KEY basic concept is that NOT all things that cause gallbladder wall thickening are acute cholecystitis. To consider acute calculous cholecystitis, you should see stones, tense distension, and the clinical symptoms should match +/- additional secondary evidence. Many other things cause gallbladder thickening on their own, including most commonly:

  • Primary liver disease (i.e. hepatitis, with secondary GB thickening)
  • Fluid retention (cardiac, renal, cirrhosis, hypoalbuminemia)
  • Gallbladder cancer (soft tissue mass invading liver most common, polypoid lesion or diffuse soft tissue thickening also possible)
  • Chronic cholecystitis
  • Adenomyomatosis

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Biliary Dilatation (see mild to moderate dilatation of intrahepatic ducts, also here and here; and significant dilatation of the common duct to 19 mm).
  • For beginners, note that in addition to knowing the anatomy, with color doppler you can easily differentiate vessels (i.e. portal vein branch) and ducts.
  • Choledocholithiasis is present - a stone in the common duct with shadowing
  • Gallbladder is mildly distended but not tense.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Ultrasound is good for looking at the bile ducts -- to both identify dilatation and search for a cause. Sensitivity of US for choledocholithiasis varies greatly in the literature as US is very operator dependent and patient factors vary (i.e. obesity rates). Further, distal common duct is often obscured by gas so is difficult to assess.

Choledocholithiasis on US:

  • Direct signs - echogenic intraductal focus with posterior acoustic shadowing (like our case), may have twinkle artifact
  • Indirect signs - biliary dilatation (same thresholds as described in CT course for common duct -- normally < 6 mm but add 1 mm per decade after 60 yo, greater allowance post-cholecystectomy).
  • When dilated, morphology of the duct is important post-cholecystectomy --> look for tapering inferiorly in the intrapancreatic portion of the duct.
  • However, choledocholithiasis can be present without dilatation (can often see stones incidentally)

Complications:

  • Ascending Cholangitis - a clinical diagnosis, but you can help by identifying dilatation, the cause (i.e. stone), and any secondary findings of inflammation (wall thickening, debris, etc)
  • Collections (i.e. intrahepatic abscesses)


IMPORTANT: Like for all imaging, the pre-test probability matters. In a patient with elevated liver enzymes in an obstructive pattern (very high ALP, bili may be elevated, more mild AST and ALT elevations), pre-test probability increases!

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 3 - Left distal obstructing ureteric stone with hydronephrosis

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate left hydronephrosis (dilated calyces + renal pelvis)
  • 8 mm distal left ureteric stone causing obstruction with posterior acoustic clean shadowing and color comet tail
  • 4 mm non-obstructing stone, left lower pole calyx also noted

Findings are in keeping with obstructing LEFT ureteric stone with mild upstream hydronephrosis and hydroureter.


Other findings:

  • 1.8 cm echogenic lesion in segment 7 of the liver, and 2.8 cm echogenic lesion in segment 8 of liver, both hemangiomas (benign)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Patients with ureteric calculi (stones) often present with 'renal colic' - which refers to the clinical presentation (i.e. flank pain radiating to the groin and coming in waves (colicky), often with hematuria).

Non-contrast CT is the gold standard test for renal colic. But, starting with ultrasound can avoid CT in certain patients -- reducing radiation dose and cost. You may also identify renal and ureteric calculi on studies done for other reasons, so need to be able to identify the associated findings.

Sonographic Findings:

  • Renal Stones (including non-obstructing calyceal stones) - echogenic foci, often with 'twinkle artifact' +/- comet tail, acoustic shadowing
  • Ureteric calculi may be difficult to see given difficulty following the ureters on ultrasound. In the setting of hydronephrosis, usually the proximal ureter near the UPJ, and the distal ureter at the UVJ at the level of the bladder are well seen. Mid ureter is harder to see.

The sensitivity of ultrasound for stones is lower than CT. Small stones may not be seen; and if they don't produce shadowing it can be hard to differentiate stone vs calcification vs vascular calcification -- the location is the key differentiator.

  • Hydronephrosis - dilated renal pelvis (pelviectasis) AND calyces (caliectasis). Can be mild if dilatation is only mild; moderate if dilatation is moderate (with blunting of fornices); and severe if there is gross dilatation/ballooning with cortical thinning associated
  • Ureteric jets - can be looked for in the bladder. Presence does NOT exclude an obstructing stone... but they may be seen less often.

teachingSections.name: Introduction to Posterior Acoustic Shadowing/Enhancement
teachingSections.html:
This is a general principle that is important in ultrasound. It is an 'artifact' that needs to be understood as there are clinical implications.

  • Shadowing - a lack of signal deep to a structure/interface. Most or all of the sound wave is reflected or absorbed by the tissue. It occurs at hard structures (i.e. bone, calcification), or at an interface between two materials that have a high acoustic impedance mismatch (i.e. presence of gas).

Clinically, consider CLEAN SHADOWING to represent some hard material - i.e. bone, calcified stone, etc.. You would expect clean shadowing behind a large renal stone for example, like in our case.

Dirty shadowing occurs behind gas, and only occasionally seen with calcification when it is less dense - i.e. in a case of porcelain gallbladder you will see clean shadowing if more dense calcification and dirty if it's a thin layer.

But, the takeaway for beginners should be dirty shadowing = gas until proven otherwise; clean shadowing = calcified/hard material. In this case, the calcified stone produced clean shadowing

  • Posterior acoustic Enhancement = increased echoes deep to things that transmit sound very well

You most commonly see this behind cystic structures (i.e. bladder, cystic lesion), but also very homogeneous solid things like lymphoma.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bulky heterogeneous pancreas (2.7 cm)
  • No cholelithiasis, no biliary dilatation

This patient had known pancreatitis. Typical features on ultrasound described:

  • Increased volume (>2.4 cm in diameter, appears bulky)
  • Increased heterogeneity, often more hypoechoic with fluid
  • Peripancreatic changes as described above.

KEY POINT: Ultrasound is very insensitive for this. The point of ultrasound is to look for gallstones and/or complications in a patient with known pancreatitis. We are just illustrating the findings so you are aware in case the pancreas is well seen and findings are typical -- you can suggest the diagnosis as discussed above.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Most importantly, acute pancreatitis is NOT a diagnosis that is often made on ultrasound. See CT case on acute pancreatitis for introduction to pancreatitis and imaging.

A diagnosis of pancreatitis is based on 2 of 3 of: elevated pancreatic enzymes, clinical history, imaging evidence.

Ultrasound is not sensitive for picking up findings of pancreatitis. It's primary purpose in the setting of pancreatitis is to look for the cause -- i.e. look for cholelithiasis. You may identify complications (i.e. thrombosis, collections).

With that being said, occasionally you will notice that the pancreas is bulky, heterogeneous, and/or has secondary findings of pancreatitis associated (peripancreatic fluid, echogenic fat, collections, etc) -- you would suggest the diagnosis and then move to correlate with clinical history, enzymes, and then CT if required.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Main Findings:

  • Appendix is distended up to 1.6 cm in diameter
  • Non-compressible at time of scan
  • Echogenic fat surrounding (also see here) -- note that echogenic fat is most apparent when scanning yourself or on sweep images
  • Thick wall with increased vascularity
  • Tender with probe pressure at that site
  • No wall defect or collection adjacent
  • Trace free fluid

Other Findings:

  • Tiny gallbladder polyps up to 4 mm

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Our initial discussion on acute appendicitis can be found in our CT course. Focus on the sonographic appearance for this case.

Sonographic Findings in Appendicitis:

Ultrasound is the initial exam of choice in young patients. Imaging findings of appendicitis:

  • Dilated appendix > 6 mm with compression. Non-compressible, no peristalsis.
  • Secondary evidence - wall thickening (>3mm), increased vascularity, surrounding echogenic fat, surrounding fluid.
  • Appendicolith - presence of an appendicolith with clean posterior shadowing is also supportive but not necessary (note that GAS will also produce echogenic interface, but will have dirty shadowing or ring-down artifact associated)

teachingSections.name: Key Points:
teachingSections.html:
1. Finding the appendix. This is a skill that takes practice. Start in the right abdomen and follow the colon down to the cecum. Find the TI and the appendix should arise from the same aspect more proximally. Look for a tubular, blind-ended structure. You need to prove it is blind-ended in two planes -- or you may confuse small bowel for a blind ended structure.
  2. Measurement is done with compression. Non-compressed appendix can measure larger than 6 mm (as we see on CT frequently). Non-compressible, round, > 6 mm is abnormal and supportive of appendicitis.
  3. Gas is present in normal appendix frequently. Most appendicitis won't have gas. If the appendix becomes gangrenous in more florid appendicitis -- then you can have gas too.


Gallbladder Polyps:

There are many guidelines for this, but practically speaking in patients without risk factors, the following rule will take you far:

  • Polyps < 6 mm = no f/u required
  • Polyps 6-9 mm = follow (6 mo, 1 year, 2 year)
  • Polyps 1 cm or larger = cholecystectomy

"Risk Factors" include: PSC, age > 60, asian ethnicity, sessile morphology.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Appendix identified, thickened and distended at the tip.
  • Gas is present within the appendix (see echogenic with dirty shadowing)
  • Adjacent large fluid collection measuring 5.8 x 5.1 cm
  • Surrounding echogenic fat

Note that still images can be difficult to interpret - you should discuss with the technologist, look at sweep images to better understand the spatial orientation, and scan yourself if needed.

Here, the presence of a fluid collection beside an abnormal appendix that demonstrates thickening and gas within (gangrenous in this case), is in keeping with perforated appendicitis with a collection.

Remember that gas is common in the normal appendix. Most appendicitis does not have gas within, but once gangrenous or perforated then gas can be seen within.

Appendicitis with Perforation

See CT cases of appendicitis for initial discussion, and prior case for introduction to appendicitis on ultrasound.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Appendix is normal (< 6 mm, compressible, no inflammation)
  • Posterior to cecum, there is an outpouching (marked with ?), shown on subsequent images to be about 1.7 cm and arising from posterior colon -- this is a diverticulum
  • There is some adjacent echogenic fat, increased vascularity, and thickening

Findings are highly suspicious for cecal diverticulitis. A CT was ordered thereafter, and confirmed the diagnosis (image below, or click to see full CT). Compare the image below with this one on ultrasound.


Other Findings:

  • Tiny cysts in the liver

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
CT is the workhorse for diagnosing diverticulitis and looking for complications. However, you will see cases in practice on ultrasound -- mostly in cases performed for other reasons (i.e. r/o appendicitis, like this case), or in patients where we start with US to avoid radiation (i.e. pregnant patients, young patients, etc).


Look for signs of colonic thickening (> 5 mm), diverticula, inflammation around diverticula (i.e. echogenic fat, fluid), hyperemic wall, pericolonic abscess/collection, and tenderness at that site.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate particulate free fluid
  • Right ovarian cystic lesion measuring 5.7 cm, avascular, with lace-like internal echoes, retractile clot (notice concave margins). See below for more details

Findings consistent with a ruptured hemorrhagic cyst.

See discussion below for general intro.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Right hemorrhagic cyst with small volume hemoperitoneum

In pre-menopausal patients with lower abdominal pain, imaging is usually performed to exclude other more serious causes of pain (i.e. ovarian torsion, complications of PID, appendicitis, endometriosis, etc).

A hemorrhagic cyst is physiologic -- meaning that it can occur normally after ovulation and will disappear by the next cycle.

IMPORTANT REVIEW (PHYSIOLOGIC LESIONS):

Premenopausal patients normally have follicles visible in the ovaries (appear as small cystic lesions) --> a dominant follicle grows in the first half of the cycle --> ovulation occurs, and then you have a corpus luteum cyst in the second half of the cycle. Occasionally you can get hemorrhage into the CL cyst (a hemorrhagic cyst). This can also rupture and lead to hemoperitoneum. Nearly all of these cases are managed conservatively.

teachingSections.name: Intro to Ovarian US: Classic Appearance of Physiologic and Benign Lesions:
teachingSections.html:
As a first step, you MUST know what is physiologic (normal), and the classic appearance of benign lesions.

PHYSIOLOGIC:

  • Follicles (physiologic) - small simple cystic structures, dominant follicle can grow - most often < 5 cm
  • Corpus luteum cyst (physiologic) - crenulated thick margins, small (usually < 3 cm), "ring of fire" vascularity.
  • Hemorrhagic cyst - cystic lesion with lace-like internal echoes, can have retracted clot that mimics solid component but will be AVASCULAR and will have CONCAVE/ANGULATED MARGINS.

CLASSIC BENIGN:

  • Endometrioma - cystic lesion with low-level internal echoes, can have tiny echogenic nodules peripherally
  • Dermoids - multiple different appearances on ultrasound. Most classically an echogenic shadowing component (tip of iceburg, rokitansky nodule).
  • Others: hydrosalpinx, peritoneal inclusion cyst -- covered in MRI course in more detail.

See O-RADS US paper in Radiology for discussion of classic benign appearances of the above and associated great images. While O-RADS US, in my opinion, has its shortcomings. It does a great job at defining terminology for physiologic and classic benign lesions (i.e. only use low-level internal echoes if its an endometrioma, etc).

If unsure if hemorrhagic cyst vs endometrioma/dermoid/other lesion, can repeat in 8-12 weeks -- a hemorrhagic cyst should disappear as it is physiologic; if it doesn't then its something else!!

teachingSections.name: Ovarian Torsion
teachingSections.html:
This ultrasound was performed after a CT suggested the diagnosis, and confirmed ovarian torsion.

Imaging findings on this case:

  • Right ovary enlarged (> 4 cm, asymmetric when compared to left), with edema (again, compare to left)
  • Lack of doppler flow in this case. However, doppler findings in ovarian torsion are variable (could be no venous flow but maintained arterial flow, absent or high resistance arterial flow, normal vascularity)
  • Twisted pedicle/'whirlpool sign' as seen on prior CT
  • Underlying ovarian lesion may be present
  • Free fluid

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html:
Adnexal Torsion

Rotation of ovary/tube along with vascular pedicle --> vascular compromise. This is a gyne emergency that is managed with urgent/emergent surgery.

Most cases occur due to presence of a lesion in the ovary (i.e. mass) that predisposes the ovary to torsion. In younger patients, hypermobility of the ovary without a mass can also lead to torsion.

Sonographic Findings:

  • ENLARGED OVARY - torsion is VERY unlikely if the ovary is normal in size
  • Twisted pedicle -- may see 'whirlpool' on sweep images on ultrasound
  • Abnormal location - an enlarged ovary in an abnormal location is torsion until proven otherwise (i.e. if left ovary is enlarged and in midline or right abdomen, think of torsion first and look for other findings)
  • Doppler abnormality - absence of flow in more severe cases (like this one), but also reduced flow/no venous flow in less severe cases.

**Normal vascularity does not rule out torsion - especially if torsion-de torsion. However, as mentioned above -- if the ovary is normal in size and position -- torsion is very unlikely.

  • Presence of a predisposing factor - usually a mass
  • Other findings - ovarian edema, enlarged ovary with peripheral small follicles, tenderness, free fluid.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right ovary enlarged, measures 6.5 x 5.7 x 2.5 cm (48 cc). For comparison, the left ovary is 21 cc.
  • Abnormal location in the posterior midline
  • Not shown here (on sweep images), there was twisting of the pedicle
  • Note there is vascularity within both ovaries, including the right (see here)

Despite preserved vascularity in the right ovary, the findings are highly suspicious for right ovarian torsion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced ovarian torsion in the last case. The key teaching points here, in addition to what we discussed in the last case are:

  • Enlarged ovary in an abnormal location should raise alarm bells for torsion -- look for supporting evidence
  • Preserved vascularity does NOT exclude torsion

RECALL: A normal size and morphology of the ovary on grayscale transvaginal US, for all intents and purposes, makes ovarian torsion extremely unlikely. So, if your technologist is having a hard time identifying internal vascularity in a normal appearing/normally located ovary that happens to be deep (and therefore harder to see flow), there is NO NEED to dictate "cannot exclude torsion". Just say there is no torsion!

teachingSections.name: Key Findings:
teachingSections.html:
Positive HCG.

  • No intrauterine pregnancy
  • Normal left ovary, containing corpus luteum
  • Left adnexal mass separate from left ovary -- measured here -- with yolk sac and embryo (CRL 15 mm), with a heartrate of 157 bpm
  • Sweep images pushing on the mass (not included) should be documented to show the mass moving separate from the ovary when in doubt - to confirm truly extra-ovarian.

The presence of an extra-ovarian adnexal lesion that is not a simple cyst in a patient with positive HCG should be considered ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise, with other findings helping increase suspicion or confirm. In this case of a live tubal ectopic, the imaging findings are diagnostic.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
All ultrasound examinations in pre-menopausal female patients should be interpreted in the context of whether the HCG is negative or positive.

In the setting of a positive HCG (i.e. pregnant), imaging and interpretation is completely different -- you will look for both intrauterine and extra-uterine pregnancy.

Ectopic Pregnancy is an extra-uterine pregnancy. It is most commonly tubal -- so on ultrasound you will see a non-simple adnexal lesion that is separate from the ovary. Other types of ectopic beyond the scope here: C-section ectopic, interstitial ectopic, cervical ectopic.

Key Teaching Points/Imaging Findings:

  • No intrauterine pregnancy (though 'heterotopic pregnancy' - which is rare - will have both intrauterine AND extrauterine pregnancy). Also note that a small cystic structure in the endometrial cavity is NOT diagnostic of intrauterine pregnancy -- i.e. you can have pseudogestational sac (a tiny cystic lesion) in ectopic pregnancy. To call an intrauterine pregnancy, you need more than a tiny cystic structure.
  • Adnexal lesion separate from ovary - usually non-simple/complex; may have solid components
  • "Ring of fire" with increased color doppler peripherally (NOTE, this alone can be seen with corpus luteum cyst as well -- BUT the key point is that CL cyst will be INTRA-ovarian; and ectopic EXTRA-ovarian -- hence the importance of knowing if the lesion is in or out of the ovary!)
  • Tubal ring sign - thick wall of tube
  • Live ectopic pregnancy is 100% specific (i.e. pregnancy with fetal cardiac activity) - like in our case. This is rare - patients usually present before this.

Presence of free fluid should be suspicious for rupture until proven otherwise.

In very early pregnancy - you will often not be able to see an intrauterine OR extrauterine pregnancy. "Pregnancy of unknown location" should be followed clinically and with repeat imaging if needed.

teachingSections.name: Right sided ectopic
teachingSections.html:
See previous case of live tubal ectopic for full discussion of imaging findings. This is another case of a tubal ectopic that is closer to what you might see in practice (rare to see live ectopic).

Findings here:

  • Normal right ovary
  • Right adnexal extra-ovarian lesion -- see here, with tubal ring, peripheral vascularity, gestational sac with yolk sac (also see here without calipers)

Findings are in keeping with right tubal ectopic


REMEMBER: In a patient with positive HCG, a non-simple cystic lesion outside of the ovary should raise suspicion for ectopic.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 13 - Cesarean Scar Ectopic Pregnancy (CSEP); C-section ectopic

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Cystic structure (gestational sac) with a yolk sac seen, along with a small embryo with a heart rate of 100
  • This is centred in the anterior myometrium of lower uterine segment, within a C-section scar. Notice thinning/relative lack of myometrial tissue peripheral to the yolk sac here

Findings are consistent with a C-section scar ectopic pregnancy.


Other findings:

  • Left ovarian corpus luteum cyst

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Cesarean Scar Ectopic Pregnancy (CSEP) is implantation of the gestational sac in the myometrium at site of prior C-section scar. It is important to identify as it can lead to severe hemorrhage, uterine rupture, death if untreated.


US Criteria:

  • Empty uterine cavity and cervical canal
  • Gestational sac implanted in lower uterine segment and anterior at the scar
  • Thin or absent myometrium between sac and bladder

**Note: Sweep images / Cine clips are key to assess morphology, as single images themselves are challenging to interpret alone.


Types:

  • Type 1: Endogenic - grows towards uterine cavity
  • Type 2: Exogenic - grows outward toward bladder with high rupture risk


This Radiographics article goes into this topic in more detail, if you are inclined.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Irregular shape of gestational sac, elongated
  • Embryo identified with CRL 16 mm, but no cardiac activity detected
  • Surrounding hemorrhage (i.e. here)

The findings alone are in keeping with early pregnancy loss.

You MUST know the criteria for pregnancy loss / refer to them in clinical practice. We cover the most important below.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html:
In pregnant patients with an intrauterine pregnancy, there are specific criteria for early pregnancy loss that you need to know or be able to quickly look up. Click here to review the criteria.

You should remember the following criteria that are diagnostic of early pregnancy loss:

  • 7 mm or larger CRL with no heartbeat (our case met this criteria)
  • MSD (mean sac diameter) 25 mm or more, but no embryo
  • No embryo with a heartbeat seen over 2 weeks after an ultrasound demonstrating a gestational sac without yolk sac; or over 11 days post ultrasound showing gestational sac WITH a yolk sac.
  • Previously documented cardiac activity no longer present

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Intrauterine mass with cystic spaces is the classic appearance ("bunch of grapes" appearance most commonly mentioned). Note that it looks like a bunch of grapes due to cystic dilatation of villi -- visible best in second trimester. This is shown nicely here on transvaginal images.
  • Internal vascularity
  • In this case there are NO normal fetal parts, in keeping with a complete mole. If some present, would be a partial mole

HCG levels are often very elevated. Treatment is usually procedural - i.e. contents are evacuated.

Follow-up is required -- of beta-HCG levels to ensure it disappears. If any residual or recurrent tissue arises, it is considered "persistent trophoblastic neoplasia" -- and managed as neoplastic (spectrum of trophoblastic tumors to choriocarcinoma). Even choriocarcinoma is very chemosensitive.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Molar Pregnancy, Hydatidiform Mole, Gestational Trophoblastic Disease

A hydatidiform mole ("Molar pregnancy") occurs in abnormal pregnancy due to genetic abnormality -- resulting in a mass occupying the uterine cavity composed of proliferated trophoblastic tissue. It can be complete (no normal fetal parts, just the mole, like in this case) or partial (only partial mole, some fetal parts seen).

Ultrasound Appearance:

  • Classic appearance: bunch of grapes due to cystic dilatation of villi
  • BUT, earlier (i.e. first trimester) the cystic spaces are smaller, which gives rise to the classically described "snowstorm appearance" - like in this case - with more echogenic confluent abnormality in addition to cystic spaces.

See this other case of partial mole below. It shows a snowstorm appearance:

And some normal fetal parts (i.e. its a partial mole):

teachingSections.name: Imaging Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Endometrial thickening seen on trans-abdominal US
  • More focal thickening on TVUS with internal vascularity within (also see this, this, etc)
  • Later images better show the more focal soft tissue within the endometrial cavity

In the appropriate clinical setting, this is in keeping with RPOC.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Persistence of fetal or placental tissue in the uterine cavity after delivery or miscarriage is considered "RPOC". Patients usually present with bleeding or pain.

The clinical setting is important in interpretation (i.e. recent pregnancy, therapeutic abortion, miscarriage, etc).

US Findings to Know:

  • Endometrial thickening (i.e. often > 10 mm) - sensitive but not specific; may be more focal as in this case.
  • Seeing material in the endometrial cavity - could be ANY of hemorrhage, avascular RPOC, vascular RPOC, or a combination
  • To figure it out, look for internal vascularity - which would strongly support RPOC. Note that absence of vascularity doesn't EXCLUDE it (given avascular RPOC can be present). But, your job on imaging is to define / identify vascular RPOC.


ASIDE: RPOC vs AVM

This comes up all the time -- and probably more than it should. Uterine AVM (arteriovenous malformation) can be acquired following uterine procedure (i.e. D+C, C-section, etc) --> high risk for catastrophic bleed. You should know the following:

  • Uterine AVM is VERY rare, so much less likely than RPOC even before seeing the images. Be careful to not overcall this...
  • RPOC is endometrial based -- the vascularity and mass are in the endometrial cavity. Since these can be vascular, they can also show low resistance high flow!
  • AVM is predominantly myometrial based, and will show low RI, high velocity, and signs of AV-shunting. Look for serpiginous channels IN the myometrium with these findings.

BUT, remember that pregnant patients also have generalized increased vascularity in their enlarged uterus. You can see prominent arcuate vessels throughout -- which should NOT be mistaken for AVM. AVM will be a cluster of myometrial vessels with shunting, high velocity, etc. If raising --> dedicated vascular imaging should be performed to confirm.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings:
teachingSections.html:
• Right testicle is heterogeneous, enlarged compared to left
  • No internal vascularity right testicle on paired testicular view (notice normal vascularity on the left).
  • Dedicated assessment of right testicle shows no vascularity

Findings are in keeping with testicular torsion.

﻿Presence of flow doesn't completely exclude torsion; but with complete torsion, there is usually absence of flow!

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Twisting of the spermatic cord can cut off the blood supply to the involved testicle -- called testicular torsion. A few things to know about this:

  • Bell Clapper Deformity - predisposes the patient to torsion. Refers to tunica vaginalis having a high attachment on spermatic cord, so the cord is more mobile / can twist.
  • Need to act fast in the setting of torsion - to salvage the testicle via surgical management.

General Sonographic Findings of Torsion:

  • Doppler findings - absence of flow in complete torsion (more than 360-540 degrees of twisting).
  • In incomplete torsion, look for altered doppler in incomplete (high RI proximal to twist)
  • Enlarged, swollen testicle - homogeneous early, heterogeneous later once infarcted (hyperechoic areas of hemorrhagic necrosis and areas of infarction can occur)
  • Twisting of spermatic cord -- follow spermatic cord on ultrasound, may see twisting or "knot" - the most sensitive and specific sign
  • Testicle may be in abnormal orientation as a result.
  • Secondary/reactive findings (scrotal thickening, hydrocele, etc)


Next Level Pearl:

You may also see the bell-clapper deformity -- i.e. if there is a reactive hydrocele and the fluid surrounds the spermatic cord nearly circumferentially!

Remember, a hydrocele is fluid between tunica vaginalis layers -- and a bell clappers deformity is insertion of this too high. See the purple in the diagram below -- you can imagine seeing fluid fully surrounding the distal spermatic cord in the bell clappers.


In cases of torsion-detorsion -- you may see either normal flow OR paradoxically increased flow! This can be difficult to differentiate from epididymitis/orchitis. Check out "Diagnostic Ultrasound" scrotal chapter for a deeper / the highest level look at scrotal ultrasound!

teachingSections.name: Key Findings:
teachingSections.html:
• Right epidydimal tail thickening and increased vascularity (also see here). Notice relatively normal body and head on the right
  • Compare with left epidydimal tail (normal thickness and no increased vascularity)

Other findings:

  • Right epidydimal head cyst, and some smaller left head cysts

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html:
Epididymitis is inflammation of the epididymis. If the inflammation extends to involve the testicle too, it is called epididymitis-orchitis (in this case, the testicles look normal).

It usually occurs due to infection, and can cause scrotal pain. Primary considerations with unilateral scrotal pain are torsion and epididymitis/orchitis clinically.

US FIndings:

  • As in our case, epididymal thickening and increased vascularity
  • The tail is often involved first / most significantly involved - the tail should normally be thin (see normal side here)
  • No associated orchitis (would demonstrate increased vascularity if associated orchitis) - see paired view to compare vascularity of both testicles

Look for complications:

  • Abscess (fluid collection, avascular) in testicle or extratesticular
  • Testicular infarct -- can be segmental areas of lost vascularity most commonly.

Next Level Pearl:

  • The presence of high resistance flow in the testicle suggests increased risk for ischemia/infarction. If global ischemia, can be difficult to differentiate from torsion but global ischemia less common.


NOTE: Sweep Images are often key for evaluating cases that you haven't scanned yourself. Still images can be difficult to interpret. When in doubt, scan yourself or review sweep images.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Increased vascularity of the left testicle compared to the right (see paired view); also see dedicated assessment of left testicle showing clear increased vascularity
  • Entire epididymis is thickened and demonstrates increased vascularity
  • Reactive hydrocele

Findings are consistent with epididymitis/orchitis.

Other findings:

  • Note scattered testicular microliths

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced epididymitis in the last case. This case shows an example of epididymitis/orchitis. Let's review what you need to know.

Clinical:

  • Retrograde ascent of pathogens (Chlamydia/Gonorrhea, or E coli)
  • Gradual onset of pain, urinary sx, fever

Imaging:

  • Epididymitis - enlarged, hypoechoic epididymitis with hyperemia / increased vascularity
  • Orchitis involved by extension (RARE to have isolated orchitis) - enlarged, heterogeneous, hyperemic (as in our case)
  • Common associated findings: reactive hydrocele (like our case), scrotal edema, pyocele (complicated hydrocele w septations/debris)
  • Look for complications: abscess, infarct (wedge shaped areas with lack of vascularity)

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 20 - Left sided epididymitis/orchitis w abscess

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Increased vascularity left testicle and left epi (background of epididymitis/orchitis)
  • Abnormal left testicular morphology with large testicular / peri-testicular complex collection with positive mass effect and containing debris. There is no internal vascularity but increased peripheral vascularity. This is an abscess
  • There is also a pyocele.

Recall that fluid between layers of tunica = hydrocele. In the setting of infection/purulent infected material here, you will see internal complexity (complex fluid, septations, debris, fluid levels etc - some of which we see here) --> think pyocele. The same is true of hematocele in cases of trauma.


We covered epididymitis/orchitis in the previous cases. This case is showing an example complicated by abscess. Management is to drain when large, patient is sick, or if ABx fail. Orchiectomy is considered when severe / testicular necrosis, uncontrolled infection.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Abnormal right testicle with increased vascularity, and intratesticular hematoma (avascular)
  • Associated with the hematoma, there is disruption of tunica albuginea with irregularity along the testicular margin with irregular, avascular extruded testicular tissue
  • Moderate hematocele

In the setting of trauma, findings are consistent with testicular rupture with intratesticular hematoma and hematocele.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Testicular Rupture is defined as disruption of the tunica albuginea (outer capsule of testis) with extrusion of seminiferous tubules. It is a urologic emergency -- urgent surgery required.

Reliable US Findings:

  • Irregular or disrupted testicular contour (compare THIS vs THIS)
  • Disruption in tunica albuginea
  • Regions of reduced/absent flow, heterogeneous parenchyma also often coexist

Other traumatic findings on US:

  • Hematoma - preserved elsewhere, no tunica albuginea disruption or irregularity (not an emergency)
  • Fracture - linear hypoechoic cleft through parenchyma but smooth external contour / tunica intact
  • Rupture (as described here)

KEY POINT: MOST IMPORTANT in testicular trauma is to carefully assess tunica for disruption, external testicular contour for any irregularity.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right groin hypoechoic saccular structure measuring 4.2 cm with internal vascularity
  • Demonstrates a classic Yin Yang on color doppler WITHIN the sac
  • To-and-fro pattern on spectral AT THE NECK of pseudoaneurysm.

Findings are consistent with a femoral artery pseudoaneurysm

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Femoral pseudoaneurysm occurs most commonly after intervention (i.e. femoral arterial access) from SFA/profunda. They present as painful pulsatile groin masses post intervention with hematoma. These are contained arterial wall breach --> sac connected to artery by a narrow neck.


US Findings:

  • Anechoic/hypoechoic sac near artery with neck connecting
  • Color doppler: Yin-Yang (swirling flow -- into the sac and then the opposite way as it exits the sac)
  • Spectral: To and Fro waveform AT THE NECK (systolic inflow is the brisk upstroke below the baseline here because pseudoaneurysm neck orientation, then diastolic outflow is above the baseline here).

Management:

  • Observe small (< 2 cm)
  • Compression (US-guided) at neck can promote thrombosis
  • US-guided thrombin injection - high success for larger sacs with narrow neck
  • Endovascular repair or surgery - failure of above, wide neck, rupture, etc.

teachingSections.name: Normal Renal Transplant
teachingSections.html:
Similar to with the liver transplant, when evaluating think of all of the things connected to the transplant -- namely, vasculature (artery, vein), collecting system; look at the parenchyma itself, and look for peri-transplant collections.

LLQ transplant, normal parenchyma

Vasculature:

  • Assess intrarenal/segmental arterial waveforms (lower, mid, and upper pole). The waveforms are normal (brisk upstroke, normal RI, etc -- see intro doppler video).
  • Renal artery patent, with normal peak systolic velocity
  • Renal vein patent

No hydronephrosis

No peritransplant collections

teachingSections.name: Vasculature/RIs
teachingSections.html:
We show a normal example here.

The most common complication you are looking for on imaging is renal artery stenosis -- which will present with intrarenal parvus-tardus low-resistance waveforms (distal to stenosis); increased peak systolic velocity at anastomosis/stenosis (>200 cm/s). See doppler intro video. Note that early post-transplant you will often see high velocity due to post-operative edema, which is normal.

Increased RIs occur in intrarenal arteries when there is something that is preventing intra-renal perfusion -- i.e. venous thrombosis, peri-transplant collection pushing on the kidney, or increased resistance in the parenchyma itself (i.e. rejection). Think of it as pumping blood into resistance.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• RLQ renal transplant
  • Intrarenal spectral waveforms are abnormal -- demonstrating a parvus-tardus morphology
  • Parvus = blunted systolic peak / low amplitude
  • Tardus = delayed/slow acceleration
  • Associated low RI (due to blunting of peak, low resistance)
  • Compare the intrarenal waveforms with the last case
  • Peak systolic velocity at anastomosis is elevated (i.e. 562 cm/s)

Findings are classic for renal artery stenosis.

Other findings:

  • Small peritransplant collections/hematomas (expected post-op)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Renal artery Stenosis post renal transplant is a complication due to narrowing of the transplant renal artery -- usually at the anastomosis. It can happen any time but peak first 6-12 months.

Imaging Findings:

  • Direct sign: Elevated Peak Systolic Velocity (PSV) > 200-250 cm/s (but higher thresholds are more specific)
  • Indirect: Tardus-parvus waveform intrarenal (downstream of stenosis), low RI

IMPORTANT PEARLS:

  • Early post-op you will see higher PSV d/t post-op edema... don't call this RAS
  • Don't rely on ONE focal high PSV. Combine focal high PSV (at anastomosis), intrarenal tardus-parvus (i.e. downstream consequence). If equivocal, can do CTA.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• LLQ renal transplant
  • Peritransplant collection/hematoma (expected post-op)
  • Intrarenal waveforms are abnormal (very high resistance waveform with reversal of end diastolic flow). High resistance also seen in renal artery itself.
  • Transplant renal vein is thrombosed (also see here)

Other findings:

  • Patient has a pancreas transplant. This is not the purpose of this case. We will discuss pancreas transplants in post-op CT and another ultrasound case.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case shows renal vein thrombosis post-transplant. More important than the diagnosis itself, I want you to recognize the clearly abnormal intrarenal waveform with very high resistance flow. Very high resistance flow post renal-transplant should make you think of situations where the arteries are pumping blood against a wall / very high resistance, which could be:

  1. Renal vein thrombosis (outflow obstruction causes increased resistance)
  2. Large peri-transplant collections compressing parenchyma
  3. Transplant kidney parenchyma abnormal itself (rejection, ischemia, hydronephrosis, etc)

teachingSections.name: Normal Liver Transplant
teachingSections.html:
This is an example of a normal liver transplant. For now, focus on vascular evaluation.

Vasculature:

  • Portal venous system is patent, without thrombosis (also see RAPV, RPPV, LPV). See normal spectral doppler (see doppler intro video for further discussion)
  • Hepatic arterial system - RHA patent with normal waveform and RI, along with LHA and common hepatic
  • Hepatic veins normal (left, middle, right)

No biliary dilatation

Normal parenchyma (no focal lesions, collections, etc)

No peri-hepatic collections seen

Complications:

The most common and dreaded complication post-liver transplant is hepatic artery thrombosis/stenosis. In thrombosis you will lose arterial flow in the artery; in stenosis, you will have REDUCED RI and parvus-tardus appearance DISTAL to the site of stenosis, and increased velocity at the anastomosis/stenosis itself. See our doppler intro video for greater discussion.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Liver transplant with small peritransplant collections (expected)
  • Portal veins: MPV, Ant RPV, Post RPV, LPV are patent
  • Hepatic veins: patent (RHV, MHV, LHV)
  • Hepatic arterial waveforms are abnormal - right hepatic artery low RI with parvus tardus, also seen on the left.
  • Hepatic artery not visualized at anastomosis, but normal more proximally

Findings are consistent with hepatic artery severe stenosis or thrombosis. Angiogram was performed thereafter, confirming hepatic artery thrombosis. Some retrograde flow of hepatic artery proper was seen via SMA circulation.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Hepatic Artery Thrombosis is a severe and dreaded complication post liver transplant. It requires urgent revascularization or retransplant -- as graft and biliary tree are threatened.


On Ultrasound, look for:

  • Absent hepatic arterial flow - definitive
  • Parvus Tardus / low RI (<0.5) intrahepatic arteries -- due to severe stenosis OR thrombosis with collaterals feeding it (like in our case)
  • If in doubt, perform CTA, or angiography.

Once more extensive collaterals form (later on), ultrasound will be less sensitive. But, clinical impact is also less severe and sometimes managed conservatively. On imaging, look closely for findings of biliary ischemia or infection.


Basic Concept: Biliary tree is predominantly supplied by arterial circulation. This arterial dependence explains why hepatic artery thrombosis --> biliary ischemia, non-anastomotic strictures, bile leaks, bilomas, infection.
